Yes, it's server-side state that gets returned with every `queue-ok` response from the AMQP server.

Actually, given the attempt of the library to have a `Queue` abstraction I think storing them on the `Queue` makes the most sense (especially since there's no way of creating a `Queue` except by `channel.declare_queue`). No. This condition is here to handle the case when the field is not required and the field is not nullable.
unmarshal_schema_object does not have enough information to make the decision on a required property since required=[] is scoped at the containing type while unmarshal_schema_object has the spec for the property. 

Side note: unmarshal_schema_object can't use the schema.is_required method since that would return the required=[] property for an object type (due to dereferencing) vs. the expected required=bool for a parameter. If the required list is non-null then schema.is_required would evaluate true. Thus we can't use the same process I did for x-nullable properties (i.e. copy of the de-referenced spec and insertion of the x-nullable property) because a boolean required property would overwrite a list property for an object type. Better would be a more extension refactoring (which I didn't have time for) where the marshaling code is also passed the type stack and can walk up the chain to check properties like required=[] and x-nullable.

Also, once the set of compared values gets this long, I'd switch to declaring a module-scope `_LOCALHOST_IPS` list and test for containment.  The check doesn't need to be inside the `try: ... except:` block either.  E.g.:


```python
_LOCALHOST_IP_ADDRESSES = [
    '0.0.0.0',
    '::',
    '127.0.0.1',
]
...
        for c in server_name:
            if c != '.' and not c.isdigit():
                return server_name
        if server_name in _LOCALHOST_IP_ADRESSES:
            return 'localhost'
        try:
            server_name = self.socketmod.gethostbyaddr(server_name)[0]
        except socket.error: # pragma: no cover
             pass
        return server_name
```

My original idea was that the user would need to make an `hduList` and write to an hdu instead of a file.  Then have a function that would add items to the header:
```
im = galsim.Image(...)
im.wcs = wcs  # which implies some header keys already
# ... make image
hdu_list = pyfits.HDUList()
im.write(hdu_list = hdu_list)
galsim.wfirst.populateFitsHeader(hdu_list[-1].header, ...)
galsim.fits.writeFile(file_name, hdu_list)
```

But I think I may have a better idea.  How's this:
```
im = galsim.Image(...)
im.wcs = wcs
# ... make image
im.header = galsim.wfirst.makeFitsHeader(...)
im.write(file_name)
```
Then the `write` function could look for a header object in image, and if there is one there, it uses that.  Otherwise it does the normal thing and lets the wcs write itself to the header.  This seems like a pretty natural syntax I think.

I'm not sure whether the wcs should also be written to the header if the user provides a header.  And which order if so. I can think of three plausible options:
1. Start by writing the wcs to the header, and then update with whatever else is in `image.header`, possibly overwriting things that the wcs specified
2. Start with `image.header`, and then let the wcs write itself to make sure that's all correct.
3. Just use `image.header` and not call the `wcs.writeToFitsHeader` function if the user has provided a header.  i.e. the user is responsible for using that function if they want it in the final header.

A [naked `except`](http://www.wilfred.me.uk/blog/2013/11/03/no-naked-excepts/) that doesn't also `raise` the Exception it caught can only lose information and submerge actual bugs. Always declare what type of Exception you expect to encounter. The python interpreter itself could throw a [`MemoryError`](https://docs.python.org/2/library/exceptions.html#exceptions.MemoryError) or [`SystemError`](https://docs.python.org/2/library/exceptions.html#exceptions.SystemError) at any time, and this code says "I don't care and I want to hide that."

Really `except` should catch a specific Exception type, or types, with only very rare...um...exceptions. :

I've tested it and it fails due to the presence of `ROOT` in the command `source lb-run LHCBDIRAC v8r2p27 ROOT`. Removing that part of the command still gives problems because the model for `lb-run` is that is not sourceable. It's the difference between:

```bash
source SetupProject.sh LHCbDirac v8r2p27; python -c "import os; print([e for e in os.environ if e.startswith(\"DIRAC\")])"
```

and
```bash
lb-run LHCBDIRAC v8r2p27 python -c "import os; print([e for e in os.environ if e.startswith(\"DIRAC\")])"
```
(Note the semi-colon and the `source` command).

The problem is that `execute()` automatically adds on the `; python -c ...` part when doing an `update_env`. It looks like using the current `update_env` will not work for `lb-run`.

I'll add another commit to this branch to make LHCb do their own, simplified version of `update_env`.

Mostly for simplicity and since current ONNX ops assume NCHW. From our experience - we usually offload the entire subgraph to backend and it can change the layout of tensors in the bigger chunk internally.

Sub-graph partitioning is not part of the proposal: its implementation it left to high-level frameworks which use the Backend API. The Backend API, however, provides information queries to help the high-level level frameworks with this task:  `ONNX_BACKEND_MACS_FP32`/`ONNX_BACKEND_MACS_FP16` and `ONNX_BACKEND_MEMORY_BANDWIDTH` information queries get information about the target backend needed to build a roofline model of performance for any known subgraph. Combined with `ONNX_BACKEND_CPU_MEMORY_READ_BANDWIDTH` and `ONNX_BACKEND_CPU_MEMORY_WRITE_BANDWIDTH`, the model would provide an estimate for the time to offloading inputs to a subgraph to the backend, execute the subgraph on the backend, and get the results back.
